stimulus:
The following text is from Frances Harper's 1872 poem “On the Cliffs.”

Oh, deep delight to watch the gladsome winds
Exultant rush among the bending pines;
Ever turned aside, yet ever pressing on—
Filled with a force that will not be denied;
To see the brilliant hues of dawn and sea.
The far white sails, glide spirit like,
Into a wider world, to feel the sweet
Enchantment of the height steal through the soul,
Clearing the burdened brain, making the heart
Leap joyous as the bright, ascending winds!
stem: Which choice best describes the function of the underlined portion in the text as a whole?

answerOptions.content: It draws a contrast between the winds and the speaker's thoughts.
It conveys the speaker's mixed feelings about the natural world.
It portrays the surroundings as a forbidding and intimidating scene.
It characterizes the cliff-top winds as a relentless and irrepressible force.

rationale:
Choice D is the best answer. The text presents the speaker's experience of watching wind sweep through pines on a cliff top. The underlined portion focuses on the idea that the winds, although “ever turned aside,” are “ever pressing on” and “filled with a force that will not be denied,” suggesting an unstoppable, irrepressible movement. The underlined portion characterizes the cliff-top winds as a relentless and enduring force.

Choice A is incorrect because the underlined portion concerns the winds, not the speaker's thoughts, and does not contrast the two. Choice B is incorrect because the underlined portion does not present mixed feelings; the surrounding lines are positive (“deep delight,” “leap joyous”). Choice C is incorrect because the speaker treats the scene with delight rather than fear or intimidation.
